sections.title: Intake
sections.conclusion: Baseline fields and source anchors establish whether the proposal can be reviewed at all.

sections.title: Claims and evidence
findings: Proposal document read by reasoning layer: yes
Requested: Annual funding for maintenance and enhancement of MLabs Core Tool Maintenance & Enhancement: Plutarch and Ply.
Recipient: Intersect Treasury Reserve Smart Contract stake address stake1784sdxt6jjennmstphgdu7l7c2scf5d02a6cve2dgn5s2kq5u3j9v, submitted by Intersect on behalf of the vendor MLabs.

Deliverables: WP1 - Plutarch and Ply Maintenance, Compatibility & Developer Experience, Critical breakages and serious vulnerabilities, Protocol-era and hard-fork compatibility, Bug fixes, correctness improvements, and optimizations, Documentation, examples, technical blog posts, and developer-experience improvements, Audit, oversight, reporting obligations, and milestone-based disbursement controls through Intersect and third parties

Claim (economic, supported_in_proposal, high materiality): The proposal requests 1,162,746 ada, consisting of 1,128,880 ada for Plutarch and Ply maintenance, compatibility, and developer experience plus a 33,866 ada Intersect administration fee.

Claim (technical, supported_in_proposal, high materiality): Plutarch is described as a Haskell eDSL for efficient Cardano smart contracts through controlled compilation into UPLC, and Ply is described as serialization tooling for Plutarch scripts and CIP-57 blueprint-style artifacts with inferred types.

Claim (adoption, proposer_asserted, high materiality): MLabs says an internal audit counted at least 26 teams using Plutarch and Ply, while the rationale later says it conservatively counted at least 15 teams building with them.

Claim (technical, independently_verifiable, medium materiality): MLabs claims Plutarch scripts rank among the smallest in its public cross-language benchmark suite while requiring comparatively low on-chain compute and memory, with a benchmark URL provided.

Claim (technical, supported_in_proposal, high materiality): The requested funding is intended to keep Plutarch and Ply compatible with Cardano ledger, Plutus, UPLC, tooling, and possible Dijkstra-era protocol changes during the funding period.

Claim (technical, proposer_asserted, high materiality): The proposer claims that if Plutarch and Ply fall behind Cardano ledger and Plutus/UPLC evolution, existing adopters could face friction, migration risk, bespoke workarounds, or avoidable rewrites.
missing: Independent evidence for: MLabs says an internal audit counted at least 26 teams using Plutarch and Ply, while the rationale later says it conservatively counted at least 15 teams building with them.

Independent evidence for: The proposer claims that if Plutarch and Ply fall behind Cardano ledger and Plutus/UPLC evolution, existing adopters could face friction, migration risk, bespoke workarounds, or avoidable rewrites.
sections.conclusion: Each claim must map to replayable public evidence; proposer assertions alone are not enough for confidence.

sections.title: Treasury analysis
findings: Six-month treasury flow regime: stressed (basis: total inflow (tau + donations) vs enacted withdrawals)
sections.conclusion: Treasury votes require a higher bar because they consume shared ADA and create precedent.

sections.title: Risk review
sections.conclusion: Risk is not a side note; unmitigated execution or governance risk can dominate an otherwise attractive proposal.

sections.title: Counterargument pass
findings: Strongest YES: the proposal substantiates "The proposal requests 1,162,746 ada, consisting of 1,128,880 ada for Plutarch and Ply maintenance, compatibility, and developer experience plus a 33,866 ada Intersect administration fee." and clears the evidence gates.

Strongest NO: a material claim is unsupported — "MLabs says an internal audit counted at least 26 teams using Plutarch and Ply, while the rationale later says it conservatively counted at least 15 teams building with them." — so cost or precedent may outweigh the benefit.
Strongest hold: if claims cannot be tied to replayable evidence, abstaining avoids overclaiming certainty.
sections.conclusion: A defensible rationale must show the best opposing case before it reaches a vote.

sections.title: Synthesis
findings: Final vote must be derived from completed sections above, not from a prose summary.
The public rationale should name the decisive section and the strongest counterargument.
sections.conclusion: The vote is only credible if the assessment tree shows enough work for a skeptical delegator to audit.
